topic.what: Data mining analysis of classical TCM textual sources to determine the historical frequency and combination patterns of acupoint selection for gastric disorders, identifying Zusanli (ST36) and Zhongwan (CV12) as the most consistently prescribed points.
topic.how: Systematic extraction and coding of acupoint prescriptions from digitized classical TCM texts and historical case records; frequency analysis, co-occurrence network analysis, and association rule mining applied to identify dominant acupoints and point combinations.
topic.why_matters: Grounds contemporary acupuncture practice for gastritis in a centuries-deep empirical tradition; provides an evidence-informed shortlist of high-prior acupoints for modern RCT design and mechanistic investigation.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Relative prevalence of top-ranked acupoints across historical prescriptions for gastric disease
characteristics.value: ST36 and CV12 consistently rank first and second across analyzed texts and dynasties
characteristics.vs_baseline: All other acupoints in the classical repertoire
characteristics.evidence: Frequency counts and ranking tables from data mining
characteristics.context: Consistency across texts spanning multiple historical periods strengthens finding

characteristics.what_it_measures: Number of clinically meaningful acupoint co-occurrence patterns identified
characteristics.value: Several high-confidence combination rules identified (e.g., ST36 + CV12, ST36 + PC6)
characteristics.evidence: Association rule mining with support/confidence thresholds
characteristics.context: Historical texts may reflect copying bias; independent practitioner variation underrepresented

good_for: Acupuncture RCT designers needing historically validated acupoint selection rationale
Mechanistic researchers choosing acupoints with the strongest historical and clinical evidence base
Medical historians studying evolution of TCM gastric disease treatment
not_for: Rare or atypical gastric presentations not well-represented in classical texts
Conditions requiring acupoints outside the gastric/stomach channel repertoire
requires: Access to digitized classical TCM corpus
Text mining and association rule mining tools

novelty: Application of data mining methods (association rules, co-occurrence networks) to classical TCM acupuncture texts for gastric disease — bridges digital humanities and evidence-based medicine
Quantitative confirmation of practitioner lore regarding ST36 and CV12 dominance
open_problems: Potential transcription and copying bias in historical corpora not fully addressed
No direct correlation established between historically frequent acupoints and modern neurophysiological evidence
Differential effectiveness of historically identified combinations not tested in prospective trials
